symptoms.symptom_name: Chronic dull dragging heavy ache in the left hemiscrotum, worse with standing/lifting

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Positional, gravity/exertion-dependent dragging ache is the classic symptomatic presentation of a varicocele.

symptoms.symptom_name: Soft compressible worm-like scrotal mass that enlarges on standing and empties supine

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Gravity-dependent emptying and a 'bag of worms' texture are characteristic; still requires exam confirmation.
A compressible, gravity-emptying mass is against a solid tumour — but consistency cannot be confirmed remotely, so a tumour is not excluded.
A new scrotal mass mandates exclusion of a solid testicular tumour by exam + ultrasound; this is why the case floors at in-person assessment.

symptoms.symptom_name: Compressible mass superior to the testicle, tortuous venous structures ('bag of worms') on palpation with Valsalva augmentation and supine decompression

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: The Valsalva-augmented, supine-decompressing tortuous plexus above a normal testicle is the classic examination finding — but it requires a clinician's hands and cannot be obtained via telehealth.

symptoms.symptom_name: No dysuria or lower urinary tract symptoms

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of LUTS argues against an infective epididymo-orchitis.
Afebrile and systemically well argues against acute scrotal infection.

symptoms.symptom_name: No sudden acute severe testicular pain; testicle not hard/exquisitely tender

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Gradual onset without sudden agonising pain argues strongly against torsion, though it can never be fully excluded remotely and must be safety-netted.

symptoms.symptom_name: Reduced ipsilateral (left) testicular volume relative to the right

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Testicular volume loss reflects chronic venous stasis and has fertility implications; a clinician measurement, not available via telehealth.

symptoms.symptom_name: Scrotal colour Doppler ultrasound showing dilated tortuous pampiniform veins with flow reversal

investigation_details.expected_result: Multiple dilated tortuous veins of the pampiniform plexus (>2-3mm) with retrograde flow on Valsalva; normal underlying testis; used to grade the varicocele and exclude an intratesticular mass.
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: Varicocele not graded and, more importantly, a solid intratesticular tumour not excluded.

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Doppler confirmation of a dilated refluxing pampiniform plexus is the confirmatory investigation — a future imaging result, never available in real time via telehealth.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: The dragging ache, the compressible worm-like mass enlarging on standing/Valsalva, and supine decompression together are characteristic of a symptomatic varicocele.

investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Characterises the mass, grades the varicocele, and excludes a solid intratesticular tumour; the mass cannot be cleared without it.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: In-person scrotal and inguinal examination (palpation, Valsalva, transillumination, cough impulse)
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Required to distinguish a benign compressible varicocele from a solid tumour or an incarcerated inguinal hernia — the examination Breath-Ezy cannot perform.
Not a first-line telehealth action and not indicated unless in-person exam/ultrasound raises suspicion of a testicular tumour; ordering them blindly over chat is inappropriate.
